## Send this feedback in Cursor Plan Mode

```text
REVISE THE PLAN BEFORE EXECUTION — KEEP THIS TASK SIMPLE AND PREPARE THE
MEMORY PLATFORM

The exploration is accepted, but the current plan is too broad.

This task must reconcile authority, global rules, project-governance templates,
per-IDE rule profiles, and validators. It must not become a separate runtime
platform before the memory/context/database system is built.

The primary goal is:

1. Remove every stale or competing instruction that could change how a future
   Cursor agent understands the project.
2. Establish one concise authority hierarchy.
3. Hardcode the New Project, Milestone, Macro/Micro checklist, Context Fabric,
   Arabold Docs, and tool-audit standards.
4. Create IDE-specific renderings of one canonical global rule set.
5. Produce an implementation-ready memory-platform handoff.
6. Stop so the memory/context/database build can begin.

Do not implement the full dynamic Tool Lifecycle Manager in this task.

RATIONALE

The memory platform will make PostgreSQL canonical for:

- project identity
- worktree identity
- agent/client/session identity
- capability profiles
- tool leases
- lease expiry
- audit events
- concurrency and claims

Implementing per-project virtual keys, JIT leases, usage ledgers, and dynamic tool
state in YAML/JSON before those tables exist would create a temporary competing
authority that must later be migrated or deleted.

The dynamic tool-lifecycle mechanism belongs in an early Milestone of the memory
platform, after the identity and policy database foundation exists.

REVISED SCOPE

===============================================================================
PHASE 0 — PRESERVE THE INHERITED WORK
===============================================================================

Keep the existing Phase 0 approach:

- inventory and classify all inherited changes
- verify the swarm.zip deletion
- run secret, junk, generated-file and oversized-file scans
- create the inherited-state manifest
- checkpoint the inherited work in a separate commit
- continue on task/authority-reconciliation

Do not mix the inherited checkpoint with the new reconciliation commits.

===============================================================================
PHASE 1 — ONE AUTHORITY HIERARCHY
===============================================================================

Create this exact hierarchy:

1. PROJECT_ANCHOR.md
   Stable constitution and non-negotiable boundaries.

2. DOC_AUTHORITY.md
   Exact document read order and classification.

3. CONTEXT_BLOCK.md
   Current mutable system state and current target architecture.

4. docs/memory-platform/MEMORY_PLATFORM_EXECUTION_PLAN.md
   Implementation authority for the upcoming memory/context/database system.

5. Current Bifrost contracts, runbooks and handoff.

6. D:\ChaosCentral-Current-Build\DOC_AUTHORITY.md
   Machine-fact authority.

No other document may override this chain.

Required corrections:

- classify the rewritten CONTEXT_BLOCK.md as current
- classify database-plan.md as historical schema evidence
- remove the dangerous H: provisioning/formatting language
- add the completed Bifrost composition:
      IDE -> agentcore-gateway -> agentcore-memory
- point all memory implementation work to the new execution plan
- state that D:\github\memory-context-database contains supporting corpus/template
  planning, not the controlling memory architecture

===============================================================================
PHASE 2 — STALE DOCUMENT RECONCILIATION
===============================================================================

Apply the repository-wide matrix.

For each stale document, perform only one of:

- update it to current policy
- add a prominent historical banner
- classify it as compatibility evidence
- classify it as Swarm-only
- replace its body with a short pointer to current authority

Do not rewrite historical evidence as current.

Prioritize files that agents are instructed to read automatically:

- AGENT_DATABASE_BOOTSTRAP.md
- AGENTS.md
- CLAUDE.md
- MASTER_CONFIG_AND_PROMPT.md
- docs/GLOBAL_AGENT_RULES.md
- rules/
- .cursor/rules/
- .agents/

===============================================================================

Keep these structures simple.

PROJECT_CHARTER.md must define:

- operator goal

MILESTONES.md must define outcome boundaries.

Each Milestone requires:

Micro steps may only be marked complete when their evidence exists.

    pending

TOOL_MANIFEST.yaml records policy and desired state only during this task.

    catalogued

    milestone_active

    dormant

    forbidden

Hardcode the rule:

- all approved tools may be available for later activation
- only currently needed tools should be exposed

- turn off Bootstrap-only and completed-Milestone tools

Do not implement runtime lease activation yet.

Document that runtime leases will be implemented by the memory platform after
PostgreSQL project/session/profile/lease tables exist.

===============================================================================

Do not create a non-Swarm profile for OpenClaw/ClawX. Record it as Swarm-managed.

Use one canonical semantic policy:

    contracts/global-agent-policy.yaml

For every managed IDE create:

    IDE_PROFILE.yaml
    GLOBAL_RULES.md

    VALIDATION.md

Every IDE profile must state:

- installed application/version
- live global-rule target
- live MCP config target
- supported rule mechanisms
- supported MCP schema/transports

At the top of every GLOBAL_RULES.md state visibly:

- whether Cursor may directly edit its MCP configuration

- validation requirement

Editability modes:

    direct_write
    generated_prompt
    manual_import
    unsupported
    unverified

Do not guess these values.

This task creates and validates the source-controlled profiles and rendered artifacts.

Do not edit live IDE global-rule or MCP files during this task.

Every IDE must receive the same semantic rules as closely as its product supports.

Mandatory semantic parity includes:

- AgentCore authority
- one Bifrost agentcore-gateway entry
- Swarm isolation
- New Project Bootstrap
- Milestones
- Macro and Micro steps

- Context Fabric checkpoints

- secrets policy

- Git safety and validation

Document all product-specific omissions explicitly.

===============================================================================

Create only the contracts needed to describe and validate the policy.

Do not create a new runtime Python platform.

Suggested contracts:

    contracts/project-tool-lifecycle.json

Create matching schemas.

Extend the existing validator system rather than inventing a large unrelated
framework.

Add deterministic checks for:

- direct IDE SQL guidance
- stale drive roles

- root/IDE policy drift
- missing IDE profile folders
- missing editability declarations
- silent mandatory-rule omissions
- missing Project Charter/Milestones/Tool Manifest in managed project fixtures
- Milestones without entry/exit criteria
- completed checklist items without evidence
- Milestones without Context Fabric, Arabold and tool-audit checkpoints
- broad wildcard grants without a documented transitional exception
- whole-drive filesystem recommendations

A small test harness is allowed only where needed to validate the contracts and
renderers.

Do not build:

- MCP lifecycle-management tools

identity and policy state exist.
```

## ChatGPT

## Locked architecture decision

Use **Cognee, not Mem0, for v1**—but only as the curated semantic/knowledge-graph layer.

Neither Cognee nor Mem0 should own the “lossless” memory. That must be built directly into AgentCore:

```text
Immutable PostgreSQL evidence ledger
        ↓
Hierarchical summaries and compaction
        ↓
Exact links back to original evidence
        ↓
Token-budgeted context compiler
        ↓
Generated GLOBAL_STATE.md and project STATE.md
```

Cognee sits downstream and receives only promoted facts, decisions, verified fixes, patterns, and selected documentation concepts.

### Why Cognee fits better

Cognee is designed around persistent cross-session agent memory, vectors, entities, relationships, provenance, and knowledge graphs. It supports PostgreSQL for relational state, pgvector for vectors, and PostgreSQL tables for graph nodes and edges, allowing the whole curated layer to remain on the same PostgreSQL service. Its PostgreSQL graph backend lacks raw Cypher, but we do not need raw Cypher for this system. citeturn532155view0turn532155view1turn383905view0turn383905view2

Mem0 is a good personalized conversational-memory product, but its default local library uses local Qdrant plus SQLite history, while its full self-hosted server’s reference deployment uses Docker, Compose, a `.env`, PostgreSQL/pgvector, authentication, and a dashboard. Those choices create duplicate storage and conflict with the native-Windows, no-Docker, Windows-environment-variable architecture. citeturn782394view1turn782394view2

Mem0’s headline April 2026 benchmark results also include managed-platform proprietary optimizations that its README says open-source users should not expect to reproduce identically. fileciteturn69file0

**Lock this decision:**

```text
Canonical lossless memory: AgentCore/PostgreSQL
Curated semantic/graph memory: Cognee behind an adapter
Mem0: rejected for v1; retained only as a future benchmark alternative
```

Do not install both.

---

# Mental model

```text
All non-Swarm IDEs
        |
        v
Bifrost: agentcore-gateway
        |
        v
agentcore-memory
        |
        +-- PostgreSQL 18 on F:
        |     - immutable events
        |     - identities
        |     - sessions
        |     - facts
        |     - summaries
        |     - source links
        |     - queues/claims
        |     - LangGraph state
        |     - docs metadata/search indexes
        |
        +-- H:
        |     - hot content-addressed artifacts
        |     - context-compaction scratch
        |     - active models/cache
        |     - Bifrost runtime
        |
        +-- E:
        |     - original cold evidence
        |     - archived artifacts
        |     - official documentation
        |     - templates/reference implementations
        |     - PostgreSQL backups/WAL archive
        |
        +-- Cognee
        |     - promoted facts
        |     - relationships
        |     - verified decisions
        |     - curated knowledge
        |
        +-- Generated projections
              - GLOBAL_STATE.md
              - .agentcore/STATE.md
              - .agentcore/DECISIONS.md
              - .agentcore/CONTEXT_INDEX.md
```

The workstation’s i9-14900KF, 128 GB RAM, RTX 4070 SUPER, Samsung 990 Pro F: tier, P5 Plus H: tier, 10 TB E: archive tier, and external G: backup are sufficient for this design. fileciteturn48file0

## What “lossless” means

The active model context is not unlimited. The **virtual context history** is.

AgentCore must:

- Preserve original prompts, messages, tool events, decisions, outputs, and accepted evidence.
- Never replace original evidence with summaries.
- Compact context into progressively higher-level summaries.
- Preserve source edges from every summary to exact originals.
- Retrieve only the smallest relevant context packet.
- Expand any summarized claim back to its raw evidence.
- Archive old raw payloads to E: without breaking retrieval.
- Resume after process or model-context resets.

`STATE.md` is the current project truth projection—not a transcript.

All agents contribute to it through `agentcore-memory`. They do **not** edit it concurrently. The projection worker regenerates it atomically from validated database state.

---

# Rock-solid milestones

These Milestones should be locked. Cursor may freely optimize, add, remove, reorder, or split the **Macro and Micro steps inside them** based on repository and machine evidence.

Cursor may not change:

- Milestone purpose
- Milestone exit criteria
- Milestone ordering
- Core architecture
- Storage authority
- Cognee decision
- Bifrost identity
- Lossless guarantees

without explicit operator approval.

## M0 — Authority and Execution Foundation

**Outcome:** Every agent sees one accurate architecture and execution policy.

Exit criteria:

- One authoritative read order.
- Stale Swarm-first and old database instructions neutralized.
- Current machine authority referenced.
- Per-IDE global-rule profiles created.
- New Project, Milestone, Macro/Micro, checklist, Context Fabric, Arabold, and tool-audit policies established.
- This exact memory-platform Milestone plan is the execution authority.
- No live memory/database implementation begins in M0.

## M1 — PostgreSQL 18 Safety Foundation

**Outcome:** A recoverable PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector platform exists beside the preserved old cluster.

Exit criteria:

- Existing cluster inventory completed.
- Logical and physical backups created.
- At least one restore test passes.
- PostgreSQL 18 and compatible pgvector run on F:.
- Required databases and least-privilege service roles exist.
- Old PostgreSQL installation remains recoverable.
- Rollback is proven.

## M2 — Canonical Identity and Immutable Evidence

**Outcome:** Every durable operation has an identity and every accepted event is preserved.

Exit criteria:

- Machine, user, project, repo, worktree, IDE/client, agent, session, run, and LangGraph thread identities are separate.
- Append-only evidence ledger works.
- Idempotent event writes work.
- Large payloads are externalized by content hash.
- Project A cannot write Project B.
- Normal IDE agents have no database credentials.
- Raw evidence cannot be updated or deleted by normal service roles.

## M3 — Lossless Context and STATE Projections

**Outcome:** Long sessions compact without losing recoverability.

Exit criteria:

- L0 recent raw context.
- L1 event-span summaries.
- L2 session summaries.
- L3 project chronology.
- Exact source links from all summaries.
- Original long prompts preserved verbatim.
- Exact expansion works after compaction.
- Exact expansion works after archival to E:.
- `GLOBAL_STATE.md` and project `STATE.md` regenerate deterministically.
- Process interruption during compaction causes no loss or corruption.

## M4 — AgentCore Memory Gateway

**Outcome:** All non-Swarm IDEs use the completed memory system through the existing Bifrost connection.

Required compact tool surface:

```text
memory_status
startup_context
retrieve_context
append_event
propose_fact
expand_source
session_open
session_close
build_handoff
docs_search
```

Exit criteria:

- Existing `agentcore-memory` Bifrost identity is preserved.
- No IDE configuration change is required.
- Multiple IDEs can use separate sessions safely.
- Append → retrieve → compact → expand works end to end.
- Degraded components are reported clearly.
- No raw database/admin tools are exposed.

## M5 — Hybrid Retrieval and Curated Cognee Memory

**Outcome:** Relevant facts and knowledge are retrieved efficiently without creating a second source of truth.

Exit criteria:

- PostgreSQL full-text search and trigram search work.
- Selective pgvector search works.
- Official documents live on E: and indexes live on F:.
- Cognee runs natively behind `KnowledgeMemoryPort`.
- Cognee uses its own `cognee_core` database on the PostgreSQL 18 service.
- Only promoted knowledge enters Cognee.
- Raw transcripts and entire repositories do not enter Cognee.
- Cognee failure does not break evidence, summaries, exact expansion, or STATE generation.
- Mem0 is not installed.

## M6 — Durable LangGraph Autonomous Workflow

**Outcome:** The autonomous developer workflow resumes safely and verifies its work.

Exit criteria:

- PostgreSQL-backed LangGraph checkpoints.
- Resume after process restart.
- Milestone state and checklist state persist.
- Requirement/scope/architecture drift gates.
- Critic, deterministic scorer, and independent judge.
- A/B implementation only when risk justifies it.
- Human pause/resume for genuine operator decisions.
- Progressive tool disclosure and JIT leases backed by PostgreSQL.
- Concurrent projects cannot change each other’s tools or state.

## M7 — Engineering Knowledge and Templates

**Outcome:** Agents have trusted examples and predictable implementation standards.

Exit criteria:

- Engineering Constitution.
- Approved dependency catalog.
- Recipes and focused reference implementations.
- Official-source provenance.
- First two Copier templates pass admission:
  - `mcp-server-python`
  - `agent-langgraph-postgres-checkpointer`
- No random codebase dump.
- No whole-repository embedding requirement.

## M8 — Operations, Recovery, Performance, and Cutover

**Outcome:** The platform operates reliably without expert intervention.

Exit criteria:

- Native Windows lifecycle ownership.
- Backup to E: and second copy to G:.
- Restore tests pass.
- PostgreSQL, memory service, Bifrost, Cognee, and LangGraph restart tests pass.
- Resource limits prevent workstation exhaustion.
- Context assembly and retrieval latency measured.
- Security and secret scans pass.
- Old PostgreSQL remains preserved for rollback.
- Complete acceptance suite passes.
- Swarm remains untouched.

# Send this to Cursor Plan Mode

Use this instead of the previous feedback:

```text
REVISE AND FINALIZE THE PLAN — LOCK THE MEMORY PLATFORM MILESTONES

Keep the current authority-reconciliation task simple.

Its purpose is to remove stale context, establish global/project/IDE rules, and create
the final execution authority for the memory platform.

Do not implement PostgreSQL, Cognee, LangGraph, dynamic tool leases, or the memory
service during the authority-reconciliation task.

ARCHITECTURE DECISION

Lock this architecture:

- PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector is the canonical non-Swarm authority.
- AgentCore itself owns immutable evidence, rolling context, compaction, exact source
  expansion, project state, and generated STATE.md projections.
- Cognee is the selected curated semantic/knowledge-graph subsystem behind an
  AgentCore adapter.
- Mem0 is rejected for v1 and must not be installed.
- Bifrost retains the stable IDE-facing `agentcore-gateway`.
- The memory subsystem retains the stable Bifrost upstream identity
  `agentcore-memory`.
- Swarm remains independent.
- No Docker or WSL dependency for the core platform.
- No Redis, Memurai, Qdrant, LanceDB, Neo4j, or a second canonical memory store.

LOSSLESS REQUIREMENT

The implementation authority must define “lossless” as:

1. Original prompts, messages, tool events, decisions, outputs, and accepted evidence
   are durably preserved before compaction.
2. Originals are never replaced by summaries.
3. Hierarchical summaries retain exact source edges.
4. Any summary or fact can expand back to exact original evidence.
5. Old payloads may move from H: to E: without breaking retrieval.
6. Active model context is token-budgeted; durable virtual context is effectively
   unbounded.
7. Compaction is deterministic, versioned, idempotent, and restart-safe.
8. Secrets are redacted before durable storage.
9. Trust and provenance follow every event, fact, summary, and retrieval.

STATE MODEL

Create these generated projections:

    C:\Users\ynotf\.agentcore\GLOBAL_STATE.md
    <project>\.agentcore\STATE.md
    <project>\.agentcore\DECISIONS.md
    <project>\.agentcore\CONTEXT_INDEX.md

All project agents contribute through `agentcore-memory`.

They do not directly edit shared STATE files.

PostgreSQL is canonical and the projection worker writes atomically.

MILESTONE GOVERNANCE

Create the memory execution plan with exactly these locked Milestones:

M0 — Authority and Execution Foundation
M1 — PostgreSQL 18 Safety Foundation
M2 — Canonical Identity and Immutable Evidence
M3 — Lossless Context and STATE Projections
M4 — AgentCore Memory Gateway
M5 — Hybrid Retrieval and Curated Cognee Memory
M6 — Durable LangGraph Autonomous Workflow
M7 — Engineering Knowledge and Templates
M8 — Operations, Recovery, Performance, and Cutover

For every Milestone document:

- fixed purpose
- fixed exit criteria
- acceptance tests
- rollback point
- required evidence
- dependencies

Milestone purposes, exit criteria, ordering, architecture, storage ownership, Cognee
selection, and lossless guarantees require explicit operator approval to change.

Macro and Micro steps are implementation guidance, not fixed architecture.

The future execution agent is explicitly allowed to:

- add steps
- remove unnecessary steps
- split steps
- reorder steps inside a Milestone
- choose better APIs or package structures
- adjust implementation details from repository evidence
- adapt tests and tooling

as long as it preserves the Milestone outcome, exit criteria, boundaries, and
acceptance guarantees.

Do not create hundreds of speculative Micro steps now.

Create enough Macro/Micro structure to make the sequence executable, then require the
implementation agent to refine the current Milestone immediately before it begins,
using:

- repository state
- Context Fabric
- Arabold exact-version docs
- Serena
- Depwire
- Tentra
- machine evidence

The authority-reconciliation task must:

1. Correct authority and stale documents.
2. Create global New Project/Milestone/checklist/tool-audit policy.
3. Create per-IDE source-controlled rule profiles.
4. Create the final memory execution plan with the locked Milestones above.
5. Add minimal validators preventing stale authority from returning.
6. Write the memory implementation handoff.
7. Commit, push, and stop.

Do not add a temporary runtime Tool Lifecycle Manager.
Do not create temporary YAML/JSON lease authority.
Do not mutate live Bifrost.
Do not alter IDE configs.
Do not begin the memory implementation.

The memory implementation will begin only after this reconciliation passes.
```

This locks the outcome tightly while leaving Fable/Cursor free to optimize the implementation details where its repository awareness is stronger.

## ChatGPT

**No. Cursor should not format any drive or change NTFS allocation-unit/cluster sizes.** That would be destructive and is unnecessary for the current architecture.

The drives are already formatted and assigned. H: already hosts the live Bifrost runtime, F: contains the existing PostgreSQL installation/data, and E: has the cold-storage structure. Reformatting any of them would erase or disrupt working state. fileciteturn70file0

What Cursor **will** optimize during the memory-platform build:

- Put PostgreSQL 18 and pgvector on **F:**.
- Put Bifrost runtime, active cache, temporary context artifacts, and compaction scratch on **H:**.
- Put cold evidence, official docs, backups, WAL archive, and long-term artifacts on **E:**.
- Use **I:** only for disposable staging because of its poor random-write latency.
- Configure PostgreSQL memory, WAL, autovacuum, connection, checkpoint, and index settings for the 128 GB workstation.
- Create the required folders, permissions, quotas, retention rules, backups, and service lifecycle.
- Benchmark the resulting configuration and tune from measured evidence.

What it must **not** do:

```text
Format-Volume
Initialize-Disk
Clear-Disk
diskpart clean
delete partitions
change allocation-unit size
repartition drives
move the live Bifrost runtime without rollback
delete or overwrite the existing PostgreSQL 16 cluster
```

“PostgreSQL cluster optimization” is different from disk cluster size. Cursor will create and tune a **PostgreSQL 18 database cluster** side-by-side on F:. It will not change the NTFS allocation unit size.

Add this to the locked memory execution plan:

```text
STORAGE SAFETY INVARIANT

All current drives are already formatted and provisioned.

No drive formatting, repartitioning, allocation-unit-size changes, disk initialization,
partition deletion, or destructive volume operation is authorized.

This applies to C:, D:, E:, F:, G:, H:, I:, and J:.

The implementation may:

- inspect filesystem and allocation-unit facts
- create approved directories
- set directory permissions
- place workloads according to the storage policy
- benchmark application/database performance
- tune PostgreSQL and application configuration

The implementation may not attempt to “optimize” NTFS cluster sizes.

F: contains the preserved PostgreSQL 16 installation/data and is the PostgreSQL 18
side-by-side target.

H: contains the live Bifrost runtime and must not be formatted or reprovisioned.

E: is the existing cold/archive tier.

I: is disposable staging only but must still not be formatted.

Any future proposal to change a filesystem or allocation-unit size requires a separate
operator-approved migration plan with complete backup, restore proof, downtime,
rollback, and explicit identification of the physical disk by stable device identity,
not only drive letter.
```

The correct approach now is to optimize **workload placement and software configuration**, not rebuild the physical volumes.

## Locked allocation-unit plan

| Drive | Target | Action |
|---|---:|---|
| **C:** | NTFS 4 KB | No change |
| **D:** | NTFS 4 KB | No change |
| **E:** | NTFS 64 KB | Archive/cold evidence; reformat only if current audit shows it is not already 64 KB |
| **F:** | NTFS 64 KB | PostgreSQL 18/pgvector hot tier; keep if already 64 KB |
| **H:** | NTFS 64 KB | Bifrost/runtime/models/hot artifacts; keep if already 64 KB |
| **I:** | NTFS 64 KB | **Reformat required** if it is still using the audited 512-byte allocation units |
| **G/J:** | Preserve | No change |

The earlier live-audited plan explicitly found:

- **F: 64 KB — no reformat**
- **H: 64 KB — no reformat**
- **I: 512 bytes — wrong; reformat to 64 KB**
- **E: intended fresh GPT/NTFS 64 KB format**

It also explicitly said that the move-off/reformat/move-back operation was required on **I:**, while C/D/F/H retained their existing cluster sizes. fileciteturn71file9

Because E:, F:, and H: contain only about 23 MB–1 GB, Cursor should verify their live allocation sizes again. If any one is not 64 KB, it should move the small amount of data temporarily, reformat correctly, and restore it. There is no reason to preserve a wrong filesystem layout merely because Bifrost or PostgreSQL has already placed a small amount of replaceable data there.

## Add this to the memory execution plan

```text
STORAGE FORMAT AND ALLOCATION-UNIT AUTHORITY

The storage-format decision was previously audited and is part of the platform
foundation.

Required allocation-unit targets:

- C: NTFS 4096 bytes — no change
- D: NTFS 4096 bytes — no change
- E: NTFS 65536 bytes
- F: NTFS 65536 bytes
- H: NTFS 65536 bytes
- I: NTFS 65536 bytes
- G/J: preserve existing filesystems; no format

Before installing PostgreSQL 18 or placing durable platform data:

1. Read current filesystem, allocation-unit size, disk model, disk number,
   volume GUID, partition style, label, used space, and active open handles for
   E:, F:, H:, and I:.

2. Identify disks by stable physical identity, not drive letter alone.

3. Expected prior state:
   - E: 65536
   - F: 65536
   - H: 65536
   - I: 512 and therefore requiring correction

4. If E:, F:, or H: already use NTFS 65536, do not reformat them.

5. If any of E:, F:, H:, or I: does not match its target:
   - inventory the small existing contents
   - stop only services using that volume
   - copy the contents to a verified temporary location on another drive
   - preserve ACLs and timestamps where relevant
   - create hashes/manifests
   - verify the copied data
   - quick-format the volume as GPT/NTFS with allocation unit 65536
   - restore the required contents
   - verify hashes, permissions, paths, services, and free space
   - remove the temporary copy only after validation

6. I: is expected to be blank or nearly blank and should be reformatted from
   512-byte allocation units to NTFS 65536.

7. E: contains only minimal provisioned folders and may be rebuilt if its current
   allocation size is incorrect.

8. F: contains approximately 1 GB of old/current AgentCore database material.
   Preserve a verified backup before any reformat. Do not restore stale database
   runtime files into the new PostgreSQL 18 cluster; retain them as rollback
   evidence.

9. H: contains approximately 1 GB of Bifrost runtime state. Preserve its live
   configuration, config database, logs needed for evidence, virtual-key
   associations, and launch configuration before any reformat. Reinstall or
   restore Bifrost afterward and rerun its full validator.

10. Do not format C:, D:, G:, or J:.

Formatting authorization is limited to correcting E:, F:, H:, and I: to the
specified allocation-unit targets. It does not authorize changing their physical
disk identity, drive letter, partition count, or workload role without an
evidence-backed reason.

The storage foundation must be completed before:
- PostgreSQL 18 initialization
- Cognee installation
- LangGraph checkpoint creation
- durable artifact ingestion
- production memory cutover
```

So the practical expected result is:

- **I: will almost certainly be reformatted.**
- **E/F/H are verified; they are reformatted only if they are not already 64 KB.**
- Their minimal contents make correction straightforward before the real memory platform is installed.

## ChatGPT

## Decision

**Approve M1 with required amendments before execution.**

Authorized after the agent incorporates the changes below:

- **Format I:** to NTFS with a 64 KB allocation unit after final stable-device verification.
- **Do not format E:, F:, or H:**; they already meet the target.
- **Install PostgreSQL 18.4 side-by-side on F:** using a new directory, new Windows service, and port `55433`.
- **Preserve PostgreSQL 16 completely.**
- **Install pgvector 0.8.5 for PostgreSQL 18.**

PostgreSQL 18.4 is the current supported minor release, and the PostgreSQL project recommends running the current minor release. citeturn775563view0

## Required corrections

### 1. Remove the PostgreSQL 32/64 KB block-size statement

This sentence is wrong:

> “F: (64KB NTFS) supports 64KB or 32KB block”

The **NTFS allocation unit** and the **PostgreSQL database page size** are separate things. The standard PostgreSQL Windows build normally uses an 8 KB `BLCKSZ`; do not custom-compile PostgreSQL with 32 KB or 64 KB pages. PostgreSQL’s official configuration documentation describes `BLCKSZ` as typically 8 KB. citeturn750264view2

Replace D4 with:

```text
D4. Initialize PostgreSQL 18 using its standard 8 KB database block size.

The F: NTFS allocation unit remains 64 KB. Do not attempt to align PostgreSQL
BLCKSZ to the NTFS allocation unit.

Use a conservative initial workstation profile:

- shared_buffers = 16GB
- effective_cache_size = 64GB to 80GB
- work_mem = 16MB
- maintenance_work_mem = 1GB
- autovacuum_work_mem = 256MB
- max_connections = 40 to 60
- huge_pages = try
- max_prepared_transactions = 0 unless a later component proves it is required

Tune later from measured workload evidence.
```

`work_mem` applies per sort/hash operation and can multiply across operations, sessions, and parallel workers, so it should remain conservative initially. citeturn750264view3

---

### 2. Strengthen the I: pre-format gate

“Zero items at root” is not enough by itself. Before formatting, verify:

- Physical model is `Crucial CT1000BX500SSD1`
- Serial/device ID
- Disk number
- Partition number
- Volume GUID
- Drive letter
- No page file
- No BitLocker protector requiring recovery handling
- No VSS shadow storage
- No mount-point dependency
- No open handles
- No scheduled task or service path
- No hidden/system files using `Get-ChildItem -Force`

Then:

- Quick-format the existing partition only
- Preserve drive letter `I:`
- Preserve or deliberately set the volume label
- Do not clean or repartition the disk
- Run a ReTrim after formatting
- Verify 65,536-byte allocation units with `fsutil`

The apparent 0.3 GB usage is probably filesystem metadata, but Cursor must prove there is no user or application data before formatting.

---

### 3. Do not assume the existing backup scripts are usable as-is

Replace:

> “all usable as-is”

with:

```text
Inspect, dry-run, and validate each existing backup/restore script before relying on it.

Patch stale paths, database assumptions, fallback-only behavior, or secret handling
when evidence requires it.
```

The logical backup must cover the **entire PG16 cluster**, not only `agent_core`:

- `pg_dumpall --globals-only` for roles and global objects
- One custom-format `pg_dump` for every non-template database
- Database settings
- Extensions and versions
- Tablespaces
- Schemas
- Functions and procedures
- Row-count manifest for every user table

Back up `swarmrecall` and other PG16 databases for disaster recovery, but **do not migrate them into the new non-Swarm PG18 platform**. Swarm remains independent.

Use clearer backup paths:

```text
E:\AgentCoreArchive\PostgreSQL\PG16\<timestamp>\logical\
E:\AgentCoreArchive\PostgreSQL\PG16\<timestamp>\physical\

G:\AgentCoreArchive\PostgreSQL\PG16\<timestamp>\logical\
G:\AgentCoreArchive\PostgreSQL\PG16\<timestamp>\physical\
```

Do not put full-cluster backups under a misleading `pgvector\base` directory.

---

### 4. Make the physical backup verifiable

If PG16 already permits `pg_basebackup`, use it with a backup manifest and then run `pg_verifybackup`. PostgreSQL documents that `pg_basebackup` requires a replication-capable role or superuser, a matching replication rule in `pg_hba.conf`, and sufficient `max_wal_senders`. citeturn775563view2

Because PG16 is small and currently stopped, the simpler fallback is acceptable:

1. Start PG16.
2. Produce logical dumps.
3. Stop it cleanly.
4. Make a complete cold physical copy of `F:\AgentCore\database_cluster`.
5. Preserve ACLs and timestamps.
6. Hash the copy.
7. Start that copied cluster on I: using PG16 binaries and an isolated temporary port.
8. Verify it independently.

Do not weaken PG16 authentication merely to make `pg_basebackup` work.

If `pg_basebackup` is used, prefer a verifiable plain backup with a manifest; PostgreSQL supplies `pg_verifybackup` specifically to validate a base backup against its manifest. citeturn775563view2turn166634view3

---

### 5. Perform two different restore tests

The plan currently tests only a logical restore. Make the distinction explicit:

#### Physical restore test

- Restore or copy the PG16 physical backup to I:
- Start it with **PG16 binaries**
- Use an isolated port such as `55434`
- Verify database list, roles, extensions, schemas, and all user-table row counts
- Stop and remove the temporary test cluster afterward

#### Logical compatibility restore test

After PG18 and pgvector are installed:

- Restore PG16 logical dumps into disposable PG18 test databases
- Do not restore them directly into the new canonical production databases
- Record incompatible extensions, objects, permissions, and SQL
- Compare full object and row-count manifests
- Drop the disposable restore databases after evidence is captured

A one-table spot check is not sufficient because the source cluster is small enough for a complete comparison.

---

### 6. Use the official pinned PostgreSQL installer source

Use PostgreSQL **18.4**, not an unqualified “18.x” install. The PostgreSQL Windows page identifies the EDB installer as the certified Windows installer and also provides a binary ZIP option for advanced deployments. citeturn775563view0turn775563view1

Preferred order:

1. Official EDB 18.4 installer or official EDB binary ZIP
2. Verify SHA-256
3. Verify Authenticode signature where available
4. Record download source and installer hash
5. Use Winget only if it resolves exactly to 18.4 and supports the required custom paths without ambiguity

Do not install unnecessary pgAdmin or StackBuilder components unless they provide a concrete requirement.

Required isolation:

```text
PG16:
  Existing binaries: unchanged
  Existing data: unchanged
  Port: 55432
  Existing scheduled task: unchanged

PG18:
  New binary directory on F:
  New data directory on F:
  Port: 55433
  New uniquely named Windows service
```

Initialize PG18 with UTF-8, SCRAM host authentication, and data checksums. PostgreSQL 18 enables page checksums by default during `initdb`; verify the resulting state rather than disabling them. citeturn201125view0

---

### 7. Do not require TLS during M1 unless certificates are deliberately provisioned

Change:

> “SSL + SCRAM-SHA-256 only”

to:

```text
- listen_addresses = 'localhost'
- permit only 127.0.0.1/32 and ::1/128
- use scram-sha-256
- set password_encryption = 'scram-sha-256'
- deny all non-loopback connections
- validate pg_hba_file_rules
```

A `hostssl` rule works only when PostgreSQL SSL is enabled and a valid server certificate configuration exists. citeturn166634view0

For this localhost-only M1 foundation, use loopback plus SCRAM. SCRAM-SHA-256 is PostgreSQL’s strongest built-in password method and avoids the deprecated MD5 password format. citeturn166634view1

TLS can be added later if a real remote-access requirement and certificate lifecycle are introduced.

---

### 8. Add the missing third database

The locked blueprint requires exactly:

```text
agent_core
cognee_core
langgraph_state
```

The plan currently omits `langgraph_state`.

Create all three as empty UTF-8 databases during M1.

Install `vector` into:

```text
agent_core
cognee_core
```

Do not install it into `langgraph_state` unless LangGraph later demonstrates a requirement.

---

### 9. Use the locked least-privilege role model

Replace the proposed broad roles:

```text
agentcore_read
agentcore_ingest
agentcore_worker
agentcore_admin
agentcore_backup
agentcore_cognee
```

with the locked service boundaries:

```text
agentcore_reader
agentcore_event_writer
agentcore_fact_proposer
agentcore_projection_worker
agentcore_compaction_worker
agentcore_migration_executor
agentcore_cognee_service
agentcore_langgraph_service
agentcore_backup_service
```

Also create non-login ownership roles where useful:

```text
agent_core_owner
cognee_core_owner
langgraph_state_owner
```

During M1:

- Establish login/database boundaries
- Revoke default public create privileges
- Test database `CONNECT` isolation
- Test schema creation restrictions
- Store passwords only in Windows environment/credential storage
- Do not print passwords

Do **not** invent table-level grants for evidence or compaction tables before those tables exist. Those permission tests belong in M2 with the schema migrations.

Do not create a general-purpose `agentcore_admin` login for agents.

---

### 10. Pin pgvector 0.8.5 and build it using its official Windows procedure

Use:

```text
pgvector v0.8.5
```

The official pgvector documentation currently identifies v0.8.5 and gives a PostgreSQL 18 Windows build procedure using Visual Studio C++ tools, `PGROOT`, and `nmake`. citeturn229184view0turn229184view1

Required proof:

```sql
SELECT extname, extversion
FROM pg_extension
WHERE extname = 'vector';
```

Record:

- Tag
- Commit
- Source ZIP/repository hash
- Compiler version
- PostgreSQL server version
- Build output
- Installed extension version

Do not use an unverified third-party binary.

---

### 11. Do not uninstall PG18 merely to prove isolation

Replace G2 with:

```text
G2. Prove service, binary, data-directory, port, and registry/service-name isolation.

- Stop PG18.
- Verify PG16 can start on 55432.
- Stop PG16 or restore its original entry state.
- Start PG18 on 55433.
- Verify no path, service, or port overlap.

Do not uninstall PG18 solely as a test.
```

---

## Approval text to paste into Cursor

```text
M1 is approved with the following mandatory amendments:

1. Format I: only after stable physical-disk identity, hidden/system-file, pagefile,
   BitLocker, VSS, open-handle, service, and scheduled-task checks pass.

2. Quick-format only the existing I: partition as NTFS with a 65,536-byte allocation
   unit. Preserve the drive letter. Do not clean or repartition the disk. Verify and
   ReTrim afterward.

3. Do not format E:, F:, or H:.

4. Remove all language suggesting PostgreSQL should use a 32 KB or 64 KB database
   block size. Use the standard PostgreSQL 18 Windows build and its standard 8 KB
   BLCKSZ. NTFS 64 KB allocation units remain separate.

5. Inspect and validate the existing backup scripts; do not assume they are usable
   as-is.

6. Back up the complete PG16 cluster:
   - globals
   - every non-template database
   - configuration
   - extensions
   - tasks/service definitions
   - physical cluster copy/base backup

7. Preserve Swarm databases in the PG16 backups but do not migrate them into the new
   AgentCore PG18 platform.

8. Store verified backup copies on both E: and G:, with hashes and manifests.

9. Perform:
   - a physical PG16 recovery/start test on an isolated temporary port
   - a logical compatibility restore into disposable PG18 test databases

10. Compare complete object, extension, schema, and user-table row-count manifests.
    A one-table spot check is insufficient.

11. Install the exact official PostgreSQL 18.4 Windows release from the official EDB
    installer or binary ZIP. Verify download hash and signature. Use Winget only if
    it proves exact version and custom-path behavior.

12. Keep PG16 binaries, data, port 55432, and scheduled task unchanged.

13. Install PG18 in new F: paths, on port 55433, under a unique Windows service.

14. Configure PG18 for localhost only with SCRAM-SHA-256. Do not require TLS during
    M1 unless certificates and all clients are explicitly configured and validated.

15. Use the conservative workstation memory profile from the locked blueprint.
    Performance tuning occurs from measurements later.

16. Create exactly:
    - agent_core
    - cognee_core
    - langgraph_state

17. Use the locked least-privilege role names from BLUEPRINT.md. Do not create a
    general agentcore_admin login. Defer table-level grants until M2 schemas exist.

18. Install pgvector v0.8.5 using the official Windows PostgreSQL 18 build procedure.
    Enable and verify vector in agent_core and cognee_core.

19. Do not uninstall PG18 merely to prove isolation. Prove isolation through paths,
    ports, service ownership, stop/start tests, and rollback.

20. Restore the original PG16 running/stopped state after testing.

With these amendments, execution of I: formatting and the side-by-side PostgreSQL
18.4 installation is authorized.

Stop immediately if:
- I: physical identity is ambiguous
- hidden or dependent data is found on I:
- either E: or G: backup verification fails
- PG16 cannot be backed up and restored
- the installer or pgvector source cannot be verified
- an operation would alter PG16 or Swarm state beyond the controlled backup test
- any target path overlaps the existing cluster
```

With those changes, the M1 plan is safe, aligned with the blueprint, and ready to execute.

Here is some of the notes i had determine if this is missing or if we replaced it, this is worrying me though align yourself with BLUEPRINT.md and if we have to add this stuff add it to the BLUEPRINT.md, trust Cursor more than us now we are drifting seriously have Cursor validate everything we have told it and have Cursor create a mental model and and cross reference it with our PC specs and Comb, Distill and 

"For the vector/index layer, I would start with plain pgvector HNSW inside PostgreSQL 18 and keep pgvectorscale / DiskANN as an optional upgrade path, not a day-one dependency. pgvector’s own docs say HNSW gives a better speed/recall trade-off than IVFFlat but at higher memory cost, and both HNSW and IVFFlat can be tuned per-query with SET LOCAL. pgvectorscale is attractive for larger datasets and disk-based vector search, but it is an optimization layer that should come after you validate your real workload. Since Cognee already recommends Postgres + pgvector as the default shape, this keeps the stack simple.

For security and governance, keep this system localhost-first and broker-mediated. Your own inventory mentions an existing unauthenticated Qdrant exposure on 0.0.0.0; the new memory substrate should not repeat that pattern. In addition, recent research shows persistent memory itself can become an attack surface: poisoned memories can be written, retrieved later, and used to steer future agent behavior. That is another strong reason to put all writes through a broker with source labeling, quarantine rules, and explicit promotion from “raw evidence” to “curated durable memory.”

So my assessment of the “Hindsight as the memory and context and database system” hypothesis is: partially correct, but ultimately not the best fit. It is an excellent component category match, but a weaker system-of-systems match than Cognee + COMB + broker + Distill-style session management.

Cursor master prompt The prompt below is designed to give Cursor a clear target architecture while still giving it room to optimize around real repo context, measured benchmark data, and friction reduction. It reflects the evidence above: Cognee as the semantic core, COMB as the markdown/context protocol, a broker as the single MCP surface, a lossless raw-event ledger underneath, and a Distill-style rolling context layer either embedded or run behind the broker."

## Decision

**Keep the current Cursor chat.** It is fresh, has the repository context, and has already completed the M1 live audit. Starting another chat now would discard useful environment knowledge.

You caught a real omission in the standalone `BLUEPRINT.md`, but not a reversal of the architecture:

- **COMB was already adopted** in the current AgentCore context as the Markdown projection convention.
- **Distill was already retained as a reference**, not selected as a required daemon.
- The original `BLUEPRINT.md` expressed the required lossless behavior but did not explicitly name COMB and Distill or state which ideas were being adopted.
- The old research notes must **not** be imported wholesale because some contain obsolete hardware mappings, old gateway assumptions, and superseded storage assignments.

I updated the blueprint to make these decisions explicit:

[Download the updated BLUEPRINT.md](sandbox:/mnt/data/BLUEPRINT_v2.md)

Replace the repository-root file:

```text
D:\github\agentcore-control-plane\BLUEPRINT.md
```

with the downloaded file.

## Final mental model

```text
All non-Swarm IDEs
        |
        v
Bifrost: agentcore-gateway
        |
        v
agentcore-memory
        |
        +-- PostgreSQL 18
        |     Canonical identity, evidence, facts, sessions,
        |     summaries, source edges, policies, leases,
        |     LangGraph state, queues and audit
        |
        +-- AgentCore Context Engine
        |     Lossless-Claw-inspired raw preservation
        |     Distill-style deduplication, token budgets,
        |     hierarchical compaction and importance retention
        |
        +-- COMB-style projections
        |     GLOBAL_STATE.md
        |     project STATE.md
        |     active context, decisions, indexes and archives
        |
        +-- Cognee
        |     Promoted semantic and relationship memory only
        |
        +-- LangGraph
              Durable autonomous workflow and checkpoints
```

### COMB

COMB is a **projection and context-governance methodology**, not another service. Its official repository separates static from dynamic information, uses task-specific hierarchical loading, favors cache-aware reading order, and archives historical details to keep active files lean. That maps directly to generated `STATE.md`, `active-context.md`, decisions, indexes, milestones, patterns, and archives. citeturn164623view0

### Distill

Do not automatically install Distill as a daemon. Lock in the desired behavior:

- Write-time deduplication
- Token-budgeted session windows
- Hierarchical compression or decay
- Importance-aware retention
- Explicit session boundaries
- Deterministic operation where practical
- Bounded compaction overhead

Cursor should identify the **exact Distill repository**, version, license, Windows behavior, and API before M3. It may implement those behaviors natively or use a hidden sidecar only when the sidecar clearly reduces code and operational risk. Either way, it stays behind `agentcore-memory`.

That unresolved repository identity does **not** block M1.

### Vector indexing

Your earlier position was correct:

- Start with PostgreSQL metadata filters, full-text search, `pg_trgm`, and pgvector.
- Preserve exact vector search as the correctness baseline.
- Use HNSW as the first ANN index when the dataset justifies indexing.
- Keep IVFFlat as a measured alternative.
- Keep pgvectorscale/StreamingDiskANN as a future benchmark-gated upgrade.

Pgvector performs exact search by default. Its own documentation describes HNSW as providing a better speed/recall trade-off than IVFFlat, with slower builds and greater memory use; IVFFlat builds faster and uses less memory but has lower query performance. Both expose query-specific tuning. citeturn309433view0turn309433view1

Pgvectorscale complements pgvector with StreamingDiskANN, quantization, and filtered vector search, but it is another extension with its own indexing and tuning complexity. It should be added only after real AgentCore data proves a material benefit. citeturn309433view2turn309433view3

```text
9. If an active context source contradicts BLUEPRINT.md:
```

## You

what does starting a new branch do

## ChatGPT

A **Git branch is a named line of development**. It lets Cursor build the memory platform without changing the known-good authority-reconciliation history.

## Mental model

Suppose the completed M0 work ends here:

```text
A──B──C──D  task/authority-reconciliation
           ^
           M0 completed and validated
```

Creating a new branch from that point gives both names the same starting commit:

```text
A──B──C──D  task/authority-reconciliation
           └─ feature/global-memory-platform-v1
```

As Cursor completes M1, M2, and later Milestones, only the new branch moves forward:

```text
A──B──C──D  task/authority-reconciliation
           \
            E──F──G  feature/global-memory-platform-v1
```

The reconciliation branch remains parked at the last known-good M0 state.

## Why that helps this project

It gives you:

- **A stable restore point.** The completed authority cleanup remains unchanged.
- **Separation.** PostgreSQL, memory, Cognee, and LangGraph implementation commits do not get mixed with the documentation-reconciliation commits.
- **Traceability.** You can identify exactly where M1 started and which commits belong to each Milestone.
- **Safe review.** Git can compare the memory branch against the M0 foundation.
- **Easy rollback.** If M2 goes wrong, Cursor can return to the last accepted M1 commit without undoing M0.
- **Controlled promotion.** The finished platform can later be merged into `main` only after all acceptance tests pass.

## What a branch does not do

A branch does **not**:

- Copy the repository into a new folder.
- Duplicate PostgreSQL or Bifrost.
- Back up uncommitted files.
- Isolate two agents editing the same working directory.
- Change live services or IDE configurations.
- Automatically save anything that has not been committed.

A branch is primarily a **Git pointer to a commit**.

## Branch versus worktree

A **branch** separates commit history.

A **worktree** gives the branch its own physical folder.

For example:

```text
D:\github\agentcore-control-plane
    task/authority-reconciliation

D:\AgentSwarm\runs\global-memory-platform-v1\worktree
    feature/global-memory-platform-v1
```

This is safer for autonomous agents because:

- The stable checkout stays available.
- Cursor works in a dedicated directory.
- Files from two branches are not swapped in and out of one folder.
- Another agent is less likely to accidentally modify the wrong branch.
- Each worktree has an obvious purpose.

## The correct starting point

The new memory branch must be created **from the final pushed M0 commit**, not from an older `main` branch and not from the abandoned `ai/global-memory-platform-v1` worktree.

Conceptually:

```powershell
git worktree add `
  -b feature/global-memory-platform-v1 `
  D:\AgentSwarm\runs\global-memory-platform-v1\worktree `
  task/authority-reconciliation
```

This means:

> “Create the memory-platform branch using the completed authority-reconciliation branch as its foundation, and place it in a separate working folder.”

## Milestone commits on the new branch

A useful history would look like:

```text
M0 completed ──┐
               ├─ M1 storage and PostgreSQL foundation
               ├─ M2 identity and immutable evidence
               ├─ M3 lossless context and STATE projections
               ├─ M4 memory gateway
               ├─ M5 Cognee and retrieval
               ├─ M6 LangGraph workflow
               ├─ M7 engineering library
               └─ M8 recovery and cutover
```

Each accepted Milestone becomes another restore point.

So starting a new branch does not add complexity to the runtime. It protects the solid foundation you just created while giving Cursor a clean, traceable place to build the actual system.

```text
- Machine: CHAOSCENTRAL
- Operator: Tony Valentine / ynotf
- Non-Swarm gateway: Bifrost agentcore-gateway
- Memory upstream: agentcore-memory
- Canonical database: PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector on F:
- Hot runtime/cache/scratch: H:
- Cold evidence/docs/backups/archive: E:
- Repositories/worktrees/builds/tests: D:
- Disposable staging only: I:
- Second backup copy: G:
- Portable transfer only: J:
- SwarmRecall, SwarmVault, SwarmClaw, OpenClaw, and ClawX are independent and untouched.
- Cognee is selected for curated semantic/graph memory in v1.
- Mem0 is rejected for v1.
- COMB is adopted as the Markdown projection/context-governance convention.
- Distill and Lossless Claw are reference patterns for rolling context and lossless compaction, not separate canonical authorities.
- PostgreSQL remains canonical.
- STATE.md files are generated projections, not manually edited truth.

Current Milestones:

M0 — Authority and Execution Foundation
M1 — Storage and PostgreSQL 18 Safety Foundation
M2 — Canonical Identity and Immutable Evidence
M3 — Lossless Context and STATE Projections
M4 — AgentCore Memory Gateway
M5 — Hybrid Retrieval and Curated Cognee Memory
M6 — Durable LangGraph Autonomous Workflow
M7 — Engineering Knowledge and Templates
M8 — Operations, Recovery, Performance, and Cutover

- E:, F:, and H: already have 64 KB allocation units and should not be formatted.
- I: has 512-byte allocation units and may be formatted to NTFS 64 KB after stable physical disk identity and dependency checks.
- PostgreSQL 18.4 should be installed side-by-side on F:.
- PostgreSQL 16 must be preserved.

- pgvector 0.8.5 should be installed for PostgreSQL 18.
```

The shell reported exit code 1, but this was already resolved. The elevated Start-Process -Verb RunAs -Wait wrapper returned a non-zero exit to the parent shell, while the actual build inside the elevated process succeeded — the log at I:\pgvector-build.log ended with BUILD_EXIT=0 and the install completed. This was confirmed immediately afterward by querying PG18: SELECT extversion FROM pg_extension WHERE extname='vector' returned 0.8.5. The evidence is in audits/M1/pg18-service-config.txt. No follow-up needed.

```text
- a Project A write targeting Project B must fail

pg_hba.conf owns authentication rules; SQL ownership, GRANT/REVOKE, RLS, policies,
and functions own authorization.

- separate machine/user/project/repo/worktree/client/agent/session/run/thread/workflow

- queue/claim/lease/dead-letter restart recovery
```

supporting enums/functions for compaction, expansion, dedupe, artifact location, proposal review, token-budget assembly, and projection revision recording.
Rollback tested in a disposable PG18 database via the down migration.

Compaction Design
L0 remains accepted raw evidence events.
L1/L2/L3 summaries are stored in context_summaries.
Every summary has exact source edges to original evidence_events.
Compaction runs are versioned and idempotent by project/idempotency key.
Interrupted runs recover after PG18 restart via recover_interrupted_compactions.
Deduplication records duplicate-to-canonical links without deleting either original event.
Distill Decision
Distill was identified as Siddhant-K-code/distill, MIT, Go, MCP-capable (distill mcp --memory --session), with write-time deduplication, token-budget windows, hierarchical decay, and ~12ms no-LLM path. M3 chose native AgentCore implementation for now because a sidecar would add moving parts without measured benefit. Distill remains a reference/benchmark source, not a dependency or canonical store.

```text
snapshot.

      -File D:\ChaosCentral-Current-Build\scripts\Test-PCDocumentation.ps1

is generated from the classified documents, regenerate it.

Do not turn that compatibility file back into the canonical source.

AgentCore authority where architecture context is needed.

Commit and push the ChaosCentral documentation update separately.

Then return to AgentCore and add one concise rule to the Milestone exit standard:

    When a Milestone changes installed software, services, ports, drive formatting,

Record the ChaosCentral commit SHA in the M3 exit evidence or current handoff.

After the documentation sync passes, proceed to M4.
```

This keeps documentation current without adding another workflow burden: **update the PC docs only when a Milestone changes live machine state.**

```text
M4 — AgentCore Memory Gateway

Locked outcome:

Every non-Swarm IDE uses the completed memory system through the existing Bifrost
connection.

Preserve without change:

    IDE-facing gateway name: agentcore-gateway
    gateway endpoint: http://127.0.0.1:8080/mcp
    Bifrost upstream identity: agentcore-memory

No IDE configuration edit is required or authorized.

Replace or extend the existing health-only implementation behind agentcore-memory
using the completed M2 identity/evidence foundation and M3 context/projection
foundation.

Required compact tool surface:

    memory_status
    startup_context
    retrieve_context
    append_event
    propose_fact
    expand_source
    session_open
    session_close
    build_handoff
    docs_search

Do not expose compatibility aliases unless an active client demonstrably requires
them. The normal advertised surface must remain compact.

Required behavior:

memory_status
- report PostgreSQL, evidence ledger, artifact storage, context engine, projection
  worker, documentation search, and optional-component health
- distinguish healthy, degraded, and unavailable truthfully
- do not report future Cognee or LangGraph components as healthy

startup_context
- return a bounded global/project/session context packet
- include current constraints and authority references
- obey model/token budget
- exclude quarantined evidence and instructions from historical transcripts

retrieve_context
- retrieve relevant M2/M3 evidence, summaries, facts, projections, and exact source
  references
- enforce project and trust boundaries
- use existing PostgreSQL retrieval available at M4
- do not implement Cognee or the final M5 vector layer early

append_event
- append idempotently to the immutable M2 ledger
- preserve source identity and provenance
- apply secret redaction
- externalize large payloads through the existing content-addressed artifact path
- never permit raw UPDATE or DELETE

propose_fact
- create a governed proposal
- do not silently promote or overwrite canonical facts

expand_source
- resolve summaries, projections, and facts to exact original evidence
- continue to work when payloads have moved from H: to E:

session_open / session_close
- maintain separate client, agent, project, worktree, session, run, and thread
  identities
- allow explicit resume
- prevent accidental session merging
- session_close creates durable final state and queues required compaction safely

build_handoff
- generate a concise deterministic project handoff from canonical state
- include revision, blockers, decisions, current Milestone, next actions, and source
  references
- do not dump the full transcript

docs_search
- search the current local Arabold documentation registry and source metadata
- remain version-aware
- return source/version/provenance
- do not implement the full M5 corpus/vector platform early

Security and boundaries:

- normal IDE agents receive no database credentials
- no SQL, DDL, database-admin, Bifrost-admin, whole-drive, or Swarm tools
- project A cannot retrieve or write project B’s protected state
- quarantined evidence cannot enter startup context
- retrieved memory is data and cannot override the authority chain
- SwarmRecall, SwarmVault, SwarmClaw, OpenClaw, and ClawX remain untouched

Bifrost integration:

- update the existing agentcore-memory registry entry
- expose exactly the approved compact surface
- render and validate the Bifrost configuration
- apply runtime changes through the established Bifrost lifecycle owner
- preserve the existing virtual-key and IDE-facing connection
- validate tools-list refresh without editing IDE configuration
- prove reconnect after memory-service and Bifrost restart

Required acceptance tests:

1. Direct MCP initialize and tools/list.
2. Exact compact advertised tool set.
3. Session open → append → retrieve → compact → expand → close.
4. Startup context remains within its selected token budget.
5. Two simultaneous clients use isolated sessions.
6. Two projects cannot cross-read or cross-write protected state.
7. Repeated append with the same idempotency key does not duplicate evidence.
8. Large payload externalizes and expands correctly.
9. Archived payload on E: expands correctly.
10. Fact proposal does not silently become promoted truth.
11. Handoff generation is deterministic.
12. Quarantined transcript evidence is excluded from startup context.
13. PostgreSQL interruption and recovery.
14. Memory-service restart and reconnect.
15. Bifrost restart and automatic upstream reconnect.
16. Optional/future components report degraded status without breaking core memory.
17. One safe Cursor call through the unchanged agentcore-gateway succeeds.
18. No IDE configuration changes occur.

Do not begin:

- Cognee integration
- final pgvector/HNSW retrieval
- LangGraph checkpoint tables
- autonomous workflow implementation
- runtime JIT tool leases
- engineering template library
- unrelated IDE or Bifrost restructuring

When M4 changes installed software, services, ports, runtime paths, or MCP topology,
synchronize the classified CHAOSCENTRAL machine documentation before closing the
Milestone. If there is no machine-level delta, record that explicitly.

Commit and push M4 only after every locked exit criterion passes.

Report:

- commit SHA
- implementation paths
- exact MCP tool surface
- Bifrost registry change
- session and identity behavior
- acceptance evidence
- restart/reconnect results
- Cursor safe-call result
- degraded-mode behavior
- rollback procedure
- CHAOSCENTRAL documentation result

Do not stop for ordinary implementation choices. Stop only for a change to locked
architecture, an inability to preserve the existing gateway identity, loss of exact
source recovery, or a security boundary that cannot be enforced.
```

```text
Required final behavior:

1. PostgreSQL retrieval

- PostgreSQL 18 remains canonical.
- Implement metadata filtering, full-text search, and pg_trgm retrieval.
- Implement pgvector exact search as the correctness baseline.
- Add HNSW only where the actual M5 corpus and benchmarks justify an ANN index.
- Keep IVFFlat, pgvectorscale, and DiskANN deferred unless measured evidence proves
  a material benefit.
- Do not add another vector database.

2. Storage placement

- Canonical official documents and source material live on E:.
- Search metadata, text indexes, embeddings, and vector indexes live on F:.
- Temporary ingestion and embedding work may use H:.
- Do not place primary indexes or databases on E: or I:.
- Do not scan the entire E: corpus for every request; search F: first and then open
  only the selected source excerpts from E:.

3. Cognee

- Install and run Cognee natively on Windows.
- Do not require Docker or WSL.
- Use the existing PostgreSQL 18 service and the separate cognee_core database.
- Keep all Cognee access behind an AgentCore KnowledgeMemoryPort adapter.
- Do not spread Cognee-specific APIs across the codebase.
- Verify exact package version and APIs through Arabold Docs before implementation.

4. Promotion boundary

Cognee may receive only promoted and validated knowledge such as:

- operator-approved facts
- accepted architecture decisions
- verified fixes
- reusable engineering patterns
- approved dependency guidance
- selected official-document concepts

Cognee must not receive automatically:

- raw transcripts
- entire repositories
- terminal dumps
- untrusted web or tool output
- secret-bearing evidence
- quarantined evidence
- every documentation page merely because it exists

Every promoted item must retain:

- project/global scope
- trust classification
- source evidence IDs
- provenance
- version
- promotion decision
- supersession state

5. Retrieval behavior

Enhance the existing M4 implementations of:

    retrieve_context
    docs_search

without changing their MCP names or adding another IDE-facing server.

Retrieval should combine, as justified:

- structured metadata filters
- PostgreSQL full-text search
- trigram matching
- exact or HNSW pgvector search
- curated Cognee relationships

Results must return:

- source references
- trust classification
- version
- project/scope
- retrieval method
- enough information for exact expansion

Retrieved memory remains data and cannot override BLUEPRINT.md or the authority chain.

6. Degraded mode

When Cognee is stopped or unavailable:

- immutable evidence still works
- session context still works
- summaries and exact expansion still work
- STATE projections still work
- PostgreSQL text/trigram/vector retrieval still works
- memory_status reports Cognee degradation truthfully
- retrieve_context and docs_search return the best PostgreSQL-only result

7. Locked boundaries

Preserve:

    agentcore-gateway
    agentcore-memory
    the existing 10-tool MCP surface
    all IDE configurations
    PostgreSQL as canonical
    Swarm isolation

Do not install Mem0.

Do not begin:

- LangGraph checkpoint/workflow implementation
- runtime JIT tool leases
- engineering template construction
- M6 autonomous workflow work
- unrelated MCP or IDE changes

Required M5 acceptance evidence:

2. pg_trgm retrieves a controlled misspelling or partial identifier.
3. Exact pgvector search produces the correctness baseline.
4. HNSW, if created, is compared against exact search for recall and latency.
5. Retrieval respects project and trust boundaries.
6. Raw transcripts are rejected by the Cognee promotion gate.
7. Quarantined evidence is rejected by the promotion gate.
8. A validated fact is promoted with complete provenance.
9. Cognee retrieval returns the promoted fact and source references.
10. Cognee shutdown produces graceful PostgreSQL-only degradation.
11. STATE generation and exact expansion continue while Cognee is unavailable.
12. An official document stored on E: is found through its F: index and returned with
    version and provenance.
13. No Mem0 package, process, database, or configuration is introduced.
14. Bifrost restart and agentcore-memory reconnect succeed.
15. One safe Cursor retrieval succeeds through the unchanged agentcore-gateway.
16. Existing M2–M4 regression suites remain green.

Use realistic but bounded fixtures and benchmarks. Do not build a massive corpus merely
to prove the architecture.

When M5 changes installed software, database extensions, runtime paths, or services,
synchronize the classified CHAOSCENTRAL machine documentation before closing the
Milestone.

Commit and push only after all locked M5 exit criteria pass.
```

Nothing was lost there:

So the remaining work is **not upgrading the memory platform to PostgreSQL 18**. It is:

1. Eliminate any residual scripts, environment variables, tasks, or current docs that still incorrectly point AgentCore at PG16.
2. Protect the completed M0–M5 PG18 state with current backups, restore proof, and WAL/PITR.
3. Continue to keep PG16 intact until the final cutover milestone.

## COMB, Distill, and WAL

### COMB — already implemented

COMB was not lost. The blueprint explicitly adopts it as the Markdown projection convention, and M3 implemented deterministic generated projections including `GLOBAL_STATE.md`, project `STATE.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, and `CONTEXT_INDEX.md`. fileciteturn11file13

The note’s core requirement—database first, generated Markdown second, no shared hand-edited global state—is already present. fileciteturn11file0

### Distill — already implemented as native behavior

Distill was not lost. M3 identified `Siddhant-K-code/distill` and implemented the relevant behavior natively:

- write-time deduplication
- token-budgeted windows
- hierarchical compaction
- importance-aware retention
- explicit session identity
- exact source expansion
- restart-safe compaction

That is consistent with the blueprint’s decision that Distill is a reference, not a second canonical service. fileciteturn11file15

### WAL — planned but not yet implemented

WAL archiving and point-in-time recovery are in M8, so they were not forgotten. The blueprint explicitly requires:

- WAL archive to E:
- backup copy to G:
- restore testing
- reversible cutover

fileciteturn11file11

However, your concern is valid: **the completed PG18 M2–M5 state now deserves protection before M6 adds more state.** This is the one important hardening item to pull forward.

## You

is the global memory set up for all the agents. And is the rolling context working

## You

The output of this plugin was redacted.

## ChatGPT

## Current status

### Global memory: **working, but universal IDE coverage is not yet fully proven**

The global memory backend is operational for the non-Swarm ecosystem:

```text
IDE/agent
  → Bifrost agentcore-gateway
  → agentcore-memory
  → PostgreSQL 18 + Cognee + lossless context engine
```

The completed system already provides:

- Immutable, idempotent event storage
- Separate project, worktree, client, agent, session, run, and thread identities
- Project isolation
- Global and project context retrieval
- Governed fact proposals
- Exact source expansion
- Version-aware documentation search
- Curated Cognee memory
- PostgreSQL full-text, trigram, and pgvector retrieval
- Generated global and project STATE projections

Cursor was directly validated through `agentcore-gateway`, including a safe `agentcore-memory` call. Any non-Swarm agent correctly connected to the same Bifrost gateway can use the same memory service.

What is **not yet proven by the reports** is that every installed IDE—Codex, Claude Code, Claude Desktop, MiniMax, Antigravity, Mavis, and Open Interpreter—has a live working gateway connection and is actually using the memory session lifecycle. The profiles and configurations may exist, but that is different from a live per-IDE validation.

Swarm agents are intentionally excluded.

### Rolling context: **yes, working and tested**

M3 and M4 proved the rolling-context engine:

- L0 recent raw evidence
- L1 event-span summaries
- L2 session summaries
- L3 project chronology
- Verbatim preservation of originals
- Write-time deduplication
- Token-budgeted context assembly
- Hierarchical compaction
- Exact summary-to-source expansion
- Expansion after hot evidence moves from H: to E:
- Restart-safe interrupted compaction
- Multi-session chronology
- Deterministic COMB-style projections
- Atomic `STATE.md` generation with previous-version recovery

M4 additionally proved the complete flow:

```text
session_open
  → append_event
  → retrieve_context
  → compact
  → expand_source
  → session_close
```

So the **engine is working**.

The remaining distinction is automatic adoption: an IDE must call—or have middleware call—`session_open`, `append_event`, and `session_close` for its activity to enter durable rolling context. Merely seeing the MCP tools does not guarantee every conversation is automatically captured.

### Not required for this to work

M6 is still pending, but M6 is for:

- LangGraph autonomous workflow checkpoints
- Critics, scorer, and judge
- Milestone persistence
- Dynamic tool leases
- Concurrent autonomous project orchestration

M6 does not create the core global memory or rolling context. Those already exist.

```text
  session_close test succeeds

Also verify globally:
```

So the factual answer is:

Persistence Location
All durable state on PostgreSQL 18 agent_core (F:):

LangGraph checkpoints: public.checkpoints, checkpoint_blobs, checkpoint_writes — created by PostgresSaver.setup()
Workflow runs: agentcore.wf_runs — each row binds one langgraph_thread UUID to a project_id
Execution state: wf_milestones, wf_macro_steps, wf_micro_steps, wf_checklist_items
Evidence / gates / critics: wf_evidence, wf_gate_evals, wf_critic_runs
Human pauses: wf_human_pauses
Tool profiles: capability_profiles (per-project tool_name + tool_state)
A/B decisions: wf_ab_experiments
Scope baselines: wf_scope_baselines
Workflow Implementation Paths

scripts/agentcore_workflow/__init__.py       — package root (v0.6.0)
scripts/agentcore_workflow/state.py          — WorkflowState TypedDict
scripts/agentcore_workflow/db.py             — PostgreSQL helpers (wf_ schema)
scripts/agentcore_workflow/gates.py          — 7 deterministic gates
scripts/agentcore_workflow/critics.py        — critics, scorer, judge, A/B
scripts/agentcore_workflow/nodes.py          — 11 LangGraph nodes
scripts/agentcore_workflow/workflow.py       — StateGraph + PostgresSaver assembly
scripts/agentcore_workflow/tests/m6_acceptance.py — 18-check harness
scripts/memory_platform/Test-M6LangGraphWorkflow.ps1 — PS wrapper
migrations/m6/001_up_langgraph_workflow.sql
migrations/m6/001_down_langgraph_workflow.sql
audits/M6/M6-EXIT-EVIDENCE.md
Key Behavior Results
Restart/Resume: wf_runs row persists across process death; LangGraph checkpoint state is recoverable via thread UUID from PostgreSQL.

Gate behavior: All 7 gates run deterministically before any critic. Scope gate detects hash drift and fails. Security gate rejects state containing secret patterns. Migration gate blocks if m6.001 not applied.

Critic/Scorer/Judge: low risk → 0 critics; medium → 2 critics; high → 4 critics. Score formula is deterministic (same inputs → identical float). Judge produced proceed, needs_operator, and block verdicts correctly.

Human pause/resume: create_wf_pause() records the pause and sets run status to paused_human; resolve_wf_pause() sets resolution to approved and restores run to running.

Lease/tool lifecycle: JIT lease created (jit_leased state), expired after 1s timeout (dormant, removed from active tools), explicit revoke sets capability_leases.status=revoked and removes tool from profile.

Concurrent-project isolation: tool-only-for-a set in project A; get_project_tools(project_B) returns 0 rows. assert_run_project_scope() enforces at the DB level.

A/B control: Enabled only when risk_class ∈ {high, critical} AND uncertainty_score ≥ 0.5. Skipped for low, medium, and high-risk with low uncertainty.

Rollback: migrations/m6/001_down_langgraph_workflow.sql drops all 13 wf_ tables, capability_profiles, 10 SECURITY DEFINER functions, and 8 enums. M4 agentcore-memory gateway continues without change.

CHAOSCENTRAL documentation: No new Windows services or scheduled tasks. Two Python packages added via pip to system Python 3.13. No ports opened. No drive layout changes.
